coredata.dc:title: A suturable biohydrogel with mechanical matched property based on coating chitosan and polyethylene glycol shell for tissue patching
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yimei
Hu, Tian
Zhang, Hongyan
Liu, Bu-Ning
Liu, Jinwen
Li, Wei-Xiong
Song, Kui
Jiang, Gang-Biao
coredata.dc:description: The mechanical mismatch between soft hydrated tissues and sutures has become a common negative impact on wound healing process. A novel method of coating multilayer polymer shells is thus reported to improve the mechanical performance of hydrogel sutures. It is suitable for tissue patching and shows advantages of convenient, efficient, and biosafety. Specifically, a precursor hydrogel (Cu@CMC) consisted of carboxymethyl chitosan and copper modified by carbon dots was used as the inner sheath, and then bonding the precursor hydrogel sheath with toughening polyethylene glycol network by anchoring sites composited from rigid chitosan shell integrated a whole structure. Subsequently, the whole system was soaked with EtOH, and rapid dehydration of EtOH was used to accelerate the entanglement process between the two coatings by constricting the molecular chains. Finally, an ideal suture (Cu-fiber) with both toughness and rigidness was obtained. The data showed that the tensile strength and biosafety of the hydrogel sutures prepared by the new strategy were significantly improved, and the skin, liver and vessel of rodents can be sutured without secondary damage. Moreover, it can inhibit inflammation response and promote the healing process of skin wound, indicating that the Cu-fiber will become a great candidate for tissue patching.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biohydrogel
Mechanical match
Soaking strategy
Seamless tissue fixation and repair

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mouse model of dermal closure and histology
Design and fabrication of Cu@CMC/CPE sutures
In vivo incisional repair using Cu-fiber

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Innovation Platform and Talent Program of Hunan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support for this study by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 41977127 , 41671302 ) and Innovation Platform and Talent Program of Hunan Province ( 2021SK4050 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Chemical structure of Cu@CMC, Cu@CMC/CS and Cu@CMC/CP fibers. (b) Surface topography schematic of the Cu@CMC, Cu@CMC/CS and Cu@CMC/CP fibers.

Relationships between viscosity and shear rate for different gel samples at 25 °C (a) and 37 °C (b), respectively. The synthesis of fibers: (c) mechanical property, (d) Young's modulus, (e) FTIR and (f) TGA patterns.

The SEM images of (a) Cu@CMC/CS, (b) Cu@CMC/PEG, (c) Cu@CMC/EtOH, (d) surface and (e) cross section (blue line shows the coated shell) of Cu@CMC/CPE suture. (f)–(h). The corresponding elemental mapping of Cu, Cd and Pb, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a). Representative fluorescence images of live (green)/dead (red) assay of different concentration bacteria (L-929) seeded onto the fiber. Scale bar, 250 μm; (b). Cell viability of different concentration Cu-fiber after 24 h; (c). Hemolysis assays for the Cu-fiber. Inset: Representative optical photograph; (d). Cumulative copper ion release from Cu-fibers (n = 3). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Analysis of sealed tissue at day 1 post wounding closed with nylon, PLGA and Cu-fiber. Representative digital images of H& E-stained (b) and Masson cross-section (c) of the incision area (scale bar = 250 μm). Regenerated epidermis at the wound site (blue arrow) and stoma (yellow arrow). (d) The H& E-stained cross-section of internal organs (scale bar = 250 μm). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The mRNA expression levels of (a) IL-6, (b) IL-1a and (c) IL-10 in skin lesions. All results are representative of at least 3 independent experiments. Data represents the mean ± SEM. 1-way ANOVA with Bonferroni's post hoc test was used.
Illustration of the synthesis of Cu-fibers and their applications in skin repair treatment.

head.ce:title: A suturable biohydrogel with mechanical matched property based on coating chitosan and polyethylene glycol shell for tissue patching

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agriculture University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agriculture University, Guangzhou 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henry Fok School of Biology and Agriculture, Shaoguan University, Shaoguan 512005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henry Fok School of Biology and Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henry Fok School and Agriculture, Shaoguan University, Shaoguan 512005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Hematology, The First Affiliated Hospital of Jishou University, Jishou, Hunan 416000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Affiliated Hospital of Jishou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Hematology, The First Affiliated Hospital of Jishou University, Jishou, Hunan, 416000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yimei Yang and Tian Hu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The mechanical mismatch between soft hydrated tissues and sutures has become a common negative impact on wound healing process. A novel method of coating multilayer polymer shells is thus reported to improve the mechanical performance of hydrogel sutures. It is suitable for tissue patching and shows advantages of convenient, efficient, and biosafety. Specifically, a precursor hydrogel (Cu@CMC) consisted of carboxymethyl chitosan and copper modified by carbon dots was used as the inner sheath, and then bonding the precursor hydrogel sheath with toughening polyethylene glycol network by anchoring sites composited from rigid chitosan shell integrated a whole structure. Subsequently, the whole system was soaked with EtOH, and rapid dehydration of EtOH was used to accelerate the entanglement process between the two coatings by constricting the molecular chains. Finally, an ideal suture (Cu-fiber) with both toughness and rigidness was obtained. The data showed that the tensile strength and biosafety of the hydrogel sutures prepared by the new strategy were significantly improved, and the skin, liver and vessel of rodents can be sutured without secondary damage. Moreover, it can inhibit inflammation response and promote the healing process of skin wound, indicating that the Cu-fiber will become a great candidate for tissue patching.

ce:section.ce:para: Surgical sutures are a kind of fixation materials for wound curing and tissue closure. The materials used for surgical sutures are classified into two types based on their in-vivo degradation: absorbable suture materials and nonabsorbable suture materials [1–5]. The absorbable sutures had drawn increased attention due to their capacity to degrade and absorb in the body as well as their inability to cause subsequent injury when they were removed. Poly(glycolide-co-lactide) (PLGA) and polylactic acid (PLA) are two common types of absorbable sutures [6–8]. Researchers have extensively studied these materials due to their consistent ability to exert large forces with high stiffness and mechanical strength, which is primarily demonstrated in rigid dry sutures. However, the elastic modulus of the mentioned rigid dry sutures (>1 GPa) is much higher than that of soft hydrated tissues (<100 kPa), this characteristic may cause an enormous mechanical mismatch between rigid dry sutures and soft hydrated tissues, which is a critical origin of secondary injury and inflammation [9,10]. The intrinsic differences between tissues and sutures have presented a major obstacle in the development of the next generation sutures. Thus, it is significant to prepare a kind of suture that can strongly reduce the mechanical mismatch.

As the skeletons of hydrogel were hydrophilic, the elastic modulus of hydrogel can be reduced by simple hydration, thus lowering the mechanical load in the suture procedure [11]. However, the capability of hydrogel to overcoming tissue tension was limited as its high-water content and poor mechanical properties. The common method to fix this issue was coated medical sutures with hydrogels on their surface to provide a smooth condition, which was found to relieve the mechanical mismatch effectively with the tensile strength of the original suture maintained. For example, Ma et al. [12] modified the surface group of polyglactin suture through covalent bonds and then coated it with the toughening polyacrylamide‑sodium alginate (PAAM-SA) hydrogel. The PAAM-SA hydrogel coating enhanced the tensile strength, polished the surface and raised the flatness of flawless sutures. Moreover, the way to improve the mechanical properties of hydrogel sutures through processing precursor hydrogels into suture has been investigated in recent years [13]. For instance, Zhang et al. [4] produced a series of stiff and robust sutures using proteins and high concentration of glutaraldehyde solution. Ghosh's group [14] turned the precursor calcium sodium sutures into copper sodium sutures by soaking them in a Cu2+ solution, which prepared the photothermal response as well as promoted wound curing and strong sutures. These strategies improved the mechanical properties effectively. However, the side effect during the glutaraldehyde process, including great biological toxicity and high concentrations of inorganic solution could cause osmotic pressure increase, which have great interference on cells. All of these biological safety issues with the production of the sutures mentioned above restrict their use in clinical administration. In addition, coating a high elasticity and toughness hydrogel on the surface of existing commercial sutures was a proper method to improve mechanical properties, but the PAAM and its degradation products were carcinogens [15]. Hence, it's necessary to develop a safer strategy for tensile strength enhancement of hydrogel sutures.

Different from adding rigid particles [16,17], introducing dual/multiple networks [18–23] or changing the cross-linked bonding [24–26] in the process of preparation can also improve the mechanical properties of hydrogel. In this study, we describe a fabricating method that coats natural polymers on the surface of precursor hydrogel, which was inspired by the efficient coating strategy. This method produces safe and mechanical matched hydrogel sutures by changing the surface network and bonding ways of precursor hydrogel. Chitosan (CS) is the only alkaline polysaccharide with both biocompatibility and biodegradability, and exhibits strong mechanical strength in the dehydrated state [27,28]. The plentiful surface functional groups (-OH and -NH2) that the CS endow them with can be used as the anchoring sites to connect two networks. Nevertheless, CS is an inappropriate option as the tough hydrogel shells, owing to its high viscosity, brittleness, and microcrystalline structure after dehydration. To address above issues, a toughness polymer is needed to modify the shell to overcome the brittleness and optimize the surface appearance of CS. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) is a nontoxic polymer with amphiphilic properties, poor protein loading capacity, low tissue adhesion, weak cell irritation, and inflammatory reactions in vivo [29,30]. Moreover, PEG is an ideal material for toughening enclosures because PEG chains are both flexible and elastic in dry condition, which helps to maintain the proper glass-transition temperature [31]. Therefore, we selected PEG as a modifier to ameliorate the brittleness and microcrystalline structure of CS shell, to fabricate a safe, mechanical matched, and suturable hydrogel suture used for the patching process in surgery.

In our previous study, we prepared a hydrogel (Cu@CMC) consisting of carboxymethyl chitosan (CMC), aldehyde chitosan (OCS) and copper modified by carbon dots (CDs@Cu), which could return to its pristine state rapidly after repeated dehydration [11]. As the water evaporates, the hydrogel could be made into a uniform, smooth and strong dry gel fiber with a constant speed stretch of 10 mm/min. This dry gel fiber could be converted into the hydrogel state rapidly by absorbing tissue fluid, thus successfully stitching the soft hydrated liver tissues without secondary injury. Nevertheless, it requires a long time to prepare and is only suitable for the hydrogel crosslinked by appropriate dynamic bonds. In this study, an ideal suture (Cu-fiber) with CS and PEG coated was fabricated through an easy implementable soaking strategy (Scheme 1 ). The Cu@CMC hydrogel was used as the inner sheath, which contacts with chitosan via polyelectrolyte interaction. As the anchoring network, the chitosan shell enhanced the tensile strength, and provided functional groups for capturing PEG chains. Meanwhile, PEG as the toughening network, could ameliorate the fragility of chitosan while reducing the microcrystalline structure and smoothing the surface of chitosan shell. The excessive PEG was then washed off by EtOH. Moreover, the rapid dehydration of EtOH promoted the formation of entangled chain networks between CS and PEG chains. This work provides a way to design and fabricate a safe, rigid and flexible suture, which contributes to the deep understanding of coating strategy, and stimulates the development of next-generation sutures for mitigating the mechanical mismatch during tissues patching process.

Chitosan (CS, molecular weight: 60000 and 1000, deacetylation >95 %) was obtained from Sanland Chemical (California, America). Polyethylene glycol (PEG, molecular weight: 4000) and carboxymethyl chitosan (CMC, molecular weight: 60000, deacetylation >90 %) were purchased from Biobomei. Co. Ltd. (Hefei, China). 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), tetra ethylene pentamine (TEPA), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Cadmium nitrate (Cd (NO3) 4H2O), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), cupric chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O), dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), 4-formylbenzoic acid (4-FA) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). NaBH4, EtOH and citric acid were purchased from DaMao Reagent Corporation (Tianjin, China). The 5–0 non-absorbable nylon sutures and the 5–0 absorbable poly(glycolide-co-lactice) (PLGA) sutures were kindly provided by the Third Affiliated Hospital of Sun Yat-sen University. All reagents were analytically grade and used without additional treatment.

The CDs@Cu was prepared by a previous modified method [32]. Firstly, 0.02 g of citric acid was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and 0.08 mL of TEPA was added to it. The mixture was transferred into a hydrothermal autoclave reactor after being stirred for 5 min. After being hydrothermal heated at 180 °C for 6 h, a light-yellow carbon dots (CDs) were obtained. The CDs were evenly stirred with 20 mL of 0.1 M CuCl2·2H2O solution, an appropriate amount of water and 40 g of NaOH were poured into the solution to obtain 80 mL blue-purple solution. Then, 1.3 g of NaBH4 was added to the mixture under continuous stirring at 70 °C for 6 h. The obtained black particles were washed with water and anhydrous ethanol for 6 times, then dried at 60 °C in vacuum conditions. CDs@Cu was obtained and then stored at sealed bag.

In a typical experiment, 2.5 g of CS (molecular weight: 1000), 4.5 g of 4-FA, and 1.68 g of DCC were dissolved in DMSO to get 100 mL of solution. Next, the solution was added to 200 mL deionized water to settle out the products followed by 24 h magnetic stirring. After the reaction, the mixture was collected and washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol in turn, then dried for 12 h at 60 °C. The obtained yellow powder is named OCS.

Preparation of Cu@CMC hydrogel was as follows: 0.06 g of OCS was dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water followed by addition of 0.6 g of CMC under constant stirring, and then 0.006 g of CDs@Cu was added into the above mixture. The hydrogel was synthesized after a 6 h stirring procedure.

The Cu@CMC sutures were synthesized following a typical microfluidic method. The Cu@CMC hydrogel was injected into a coagulation bath containing 2 % (w/v) chiostan-1 % (v/v) CH3COOH, 30 % (w/v) PEG-4000 and 85 % (v/v) EtOH solution through thin tubing respectively, to obtain Cu@CMC/CS, Cu@CMC/PEG and Cu@CMC/EtOH sutures. For further preparation, the Cu@CMC hydrogel was injected and soaked in the a 2 % (w/v) chiostan-1 % (v/v) HCl and 30 % (w/v) PEG-4000 coagulation bath successively to obtain Cu@CMC/CP sutures, the Cu@CMC/CPE sutures were synthesized by being soaked in the above three coagulation bath in turn. The stretched gel was prepared by Cu@CMC hydrogel with a constant speed stretch of 10 mm/min. All sutures were suspended, dried for 24 h at room temperature by their own gravity, then stored in sealed bag.

Rheological test (MCR502 rheometer, Anton Par, Austria) was used to testify the injectable test of Cu@CMC hydrogel, Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FTIR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Fisher Scientific) was employed to analyze the surface functional groups of sutures, the tensile strength of sutures were measured by a universal test machine (SUST, China Zhuhai), the surface structures of sutures were analyzed by field emission scanning electron micrograph (Nova Nano SEM 430, FEI).

In a typical procedure, fresh blood of Kunmnig (KM) mice was washed with normal saline under 1500 rpm centrifugation several times to obtain erythrocyte precipitate, then diluted to 2 % (v/v) erythrocyte suspension liquid by normal saline. To get the sutures' solution, sutures' powder was added in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and oscillate at 37 °C for 24 h, 0.5 mL of diluted erythrocyte suspension liquid was added into 0.5 mL of the sample's suspension solutions in PBS at several concentrations (from 25 to 1600 ppm). Erythrocyte suspension liquid was added into PBS, while the normal saline and deionized water were chosen as negative and positive controls. All samples were incubated for 3 h at 37 °C. 200 μL of supernatant was added to 96-well plates after being centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 3 min. The absorbance value of sutures' solution was measured with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 540 nm. The hemolysis rate was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Hemolysis
%
=
OD sample
−
OD negative
OD positive
−
OD negative
× 100

In this equation, OD
sample , OD
negative and OD
positive is the absorbance value of sample, negative control and positive control respectively.

0.1 g of sutures were added to conical flasks, decanted 20 mL of PBS. Oscillate at 37 °C for 150 rpm. Centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min after sampling at the setting time points, took supernatant through the 0.22 μ m water system membrane, measured the concentration of Cu2+ with atomic absorption spectrometer (AAS, Hitachi Jaco6-25, Z2300, China). Each group was set to 3 parallel tests (n = 3).

All the samples were sterilized by ultraviolet irradiation before extract preparation. Different mass of sutures was immersed in PBS, incubated for 24 h at 37 °C under constant oscillation, to obtain different concentration of suture extract. The supernatant was collected for cytotoxicity test. Prior to adding L-929 mouse fibroblast cells (NCNT clone 929), suture extract was added to 96-well plates, and incubated at 5 % CO2, 37 °C in the 95 % humidity chamber for 24 h. Calcein-AM/PI invitrogen were used to stain the cells for 30 min, laser scanning confocal microscope (TCS SP8, Leica, Germany) was employed to count the live and dead cells.

Cell viability was adopted using the CCK-8 assay. In brief, 100 μ L of 10 % CCK-8 reagent (Dojindo, Japan) was added to the cells and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. The absorbance value of viable cells was employed to automatic microplate spectrophotometer (EON, Gene Company Limited) at 450 nm. The resulting value was calculated by Eq. (2):
(2)
Cell Viability
%
=
OD sample
−
OD negative
OD control
−
OD negative
× 100
Where OD sample, OD negative and OD control represents the absorbance values of sample treated cells, CCK-8 and untreated cells. All cell viability from 3 independent groups (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mouse model of dermal closure and histology
ce:section.ce:para: All KM mice (8–10 weeks, weights of 35 ± 5 g) were bought from Animal Centre of Guangdong Province (Guangzhou, China). Prior to the surgery experiment, three mice were divided into a group randomly. Before surgery, mice were anesthetized with 1.3 mg/kg pentobarbital sodium by intraperitoneal injection. A full-thickness dorsal skin model in mice was used to stimulate patching effect. In brief, clip the hair on the dorsal region and clean the skin with chlorhexidine gluconate and 75 % (v/v) of anhydrous ethanol. A 1-cm diameter skin incision was made by 15G needle. Incisions were closed using 3 interrupted knots of nylon, PLGA and Cu@CMC/CPE (Cu-fiber) [33]. Then placed them individually, mice were euthanized by CO2 inhalation followed by cervical dislocation at day 1, 4 and 7.

Skin from the incision site was immersed in 10 % (v/v) of neutral buffered formalin solution (Scigen) for 12 h before further histology procedure. After that, skin tissues were dehydrated by ethyl alcohol (70 %–100 %), cleared in xylene, and embedded in paraffin. Rotary microtome (Leica, RM2126RT, Wetzlar, Hessen, Germany) was employed to serially section the specimens at 5 μm. The sections were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and Masson. All histology were derived from 3 setting experiments (n = 3).

Cu-fiber was used as the experimental group, whereas nylon suture and PLGA suture were employed to be the control group. The incisions regions were removed and stored at RNA solid after cleaning the sutures after operating surgery on days 1, 4 and 7. Total RNA was isolated by using Trizol (Invitrogen, USA). ITaqTM Universal SYBR® Green Supermix (Bio-Rad) on a LightCycler 96 (Roche) thermocycle was adopted to detect the mRNA expression of Interleukin 6 (IL-6), Interleukin 1a (IL-1a) and Interleukin 10 (IL-10). IL-6, IL-1a and IL-10 expression was normalized relative to Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH) expression. All of the primers are shown in Table S1.
Data from at least three comparative experiments were evaluated using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Fisher's test. All statistical analysis was calculated by Origin 9.0 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and fabrication of Cu@CMC/CPE sutures
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by coating strategy, which encapsulate and glue commercially available surgical sutures together with toughening hydrogel, the Cu@CMC/CPE suture was prepared by soaking polymers. Fig. 1(a) and (b) showed the process of generating Cu@CMC/CPE sutures. Briefly, we illustrated the strategy with Cu@CMC hydrogel, which consists of CMC, OCS and CDs@Cu. The Cu@CMC hydrogels were injected to form a fiber. Due to its weaker capacity for patching, the pristine fiber was treated with 2 % (w/v) chiostan-1 % (v/v) CH3COOH solution to strengthen mechanical property and form an anchoring network. In order to introduce a toughening network and reform the rough surface, the modified fiber was soaked into 30 % (w/v) of PEG-4000. After soaking procedure, the 85 % (v/v) of EtOH solution was employed to remove excess PEG and catalyze the entangled chain network.

In order to testify the appropriate content of CDs@Cu in consideration of injectable capacity, the relationships between viscosity and shear rate of different hydrogel samples (content of CDs@Cu: 0.002–0.008 g) were detected. As shown in Fig. 2(a) and (b), the viscosity on all groups of hydrogels decreased with the increasement of shear rate, and the decreasing rate of 0.006 and 0.008 were more obvious than that of 0.002 and 0.004. Therefore, the groups of 0.006 and 0.008 could be easily injected at 25 °C and 37 °C [34,35]. As for group 0.008, the viscosity always larger than group 0.006 at 37 °C, with viscosity decreasing to 10−1 Pa·s, the shear rate of group 0.008 is 700 % of the group 0.006. Thus, the group 0.006 was suggested to be an appropriate candidate for the experiment.

In order to recognize the tensile impact on the soaking strategy, a universal test machine was measured to analyze the tensile strength of sutures. All tested sutures were cut into 5 cm long and clamped to the analyzer vertically. At room temperature, the sutures were stretched at a speed of 0.3 mm/min with the original gauge length of 30 mm in uniaxial stretching mode, until the suture breaks. All samples were tested 6 times and the average value of these 6 tests were shown in Fig. 2(c) and (d). The stretched Cu@CMC sutures described in our previous study were chosen as the control group because the Cu@CMC cannot solidify under its own gravity. In comparison with the stretched Cu@CMC gels, the sutures constructed by soaking strategy have a lower value of maximum stresses and Young's modulus, but were strong enough to penetrate the soft hydrated tissues. The existence of CS made hydrogel sheath become brittle, scilicet, the Young's modulus of Cu@CMC/CS significantly higher than that of Cu@CMC/PEG (809.1 MPa compared to 235.7 MPa), which indicated that CS had a better mechanical enhancement ability than PEG. About the anchoring network, the good compatibility of CS conjugated to Cu@CMC sheath and PEG shell resulted in a higher strain value of Cu@CMC/CP than in Cu@CMC/CS. Moreover, the stronger stress of Cu@CMC/CP was also higher than its in Cu@CMC/PEG. The Cu@CMC/CS and Cu@CMC/CP sutures displayed a greater stress during the stage of elastic deformation, indicating that PEG itself has a weaker enhancement ability than CS. The strain values of Cu@CMC/PEG, Cu@CMC/CP and Cu@CMC/CPE were obviously higher than Cu@CMC/CS, demonstrating that the PEG could be used as a great toughening agent for fabricating the sutures. EtOH functioned as a detergent of PEG may exert a strong dehydrating effect. Therefore, EtOH can significantly weaken the strain of sutures, which was corresponding to the decrease in strain value after EtOH therapy (12.66 % of Cu@CMC/CP and 9.7 % of Cu@CMC/CPE). Meanwhile, the mechanical strength was enhanced (570.255 MPa increased to 692.385 MPa). Besides, the stress-strain curves and Young's modulus of the commercial PLGA fiber and nylon fiber were shown in Fig. S1. The Young's modulus of Cu-fiber was slightly higher than that of PLGA fiber (611.19 MPa). Consequently, the strategy is working in addressing the mechanical issues. The Cu@CMC/CPE suture (Cu-fiber) is a great candidate for tissue patching.

In order to investigate the mechanism of above soaking strategy, FTIR were used to analyze the surface functional groups of sutures. As shown in Fig. 2(e), the profiles of Cu@CMC, Cu@CMC/CS and Cu@CMC/EtOH were very similar due to their same chemical nature. Among these samples, the signal around 3432 cm−1 and 2892 cm−1 were detected to the stretching vibrations of -OH and -CH2 groups. The signals around 1635 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching variation of -C=O group which were the characteristic peaks of CS. It should be noted that the detected characteristic peaks of -C=O of Cu@CMC/CS were much broader than Cu@CMC, indicating the CS successfully parceled to Cu@CMC sheath. Because of the same chemical reagent, both Cu@CMC/PEG and Cu@CMC/CPE have feature peaks around 2892 cm−1. The detected peaks around 1280 cm−1, 964 cm−1 and 842 cm−1 were attributed to crystal band of PEG and -C-C-O group [36,37]. The results could be attributed to the polyelectrolyte reaction between Cu@CMC/CS sheath and CS anchoring network. The main mechanism of the solid combination of CS with PEG probably was chain entanglement, which was consistent with the design mechanism.

The thermogravimetric (TGA) curves of the fibers before and after soaking process were shown in Fig. 2(f). All five TGA curves showed the similar trend, the initial weight loss was ascribed to the evaporation of the water in the Cu@CMC hydrogel sheath and the outer shell. Then followed a sharp drop in weight around 240 °C, which was caused by the release of gas molecules (CO, CO2, and NH3) of the partial pyrolysis of the CMC. Although Cu@CMC/CS and Cu@CMC/PEG were thermally degraded from 210 to 360 °C, the thermal degradation temperatures of Cu@CMC/CP and Cu@CMC/CPE were similar to the Cu@CMC/PEG and Cu@CMC/CS, respectively. Thus, it was indicated that the interaction between PEG and CS was inexistence and the elution role of EtOH. Also, it suggested that the extent of weight loss in the Cu@CMC/CS and Cu@CMC/CPE during the thermal degradation was lower than that in the Cu@CMC/CP sample at the high temperature. As a result, the thermal stability of both Cu@CMC/CS and Cu@CMC/CPE increased by the presence of the cross-link between CS and Cu@CMC.

For analyzing the impact of coagulating bath on sutures' microtopography, the sutures processed by the soaking procedure were collected and dried, and these samples were detected through SEM. The data in Fig. 3(a)–(d) described the microtopography of Cu@CMC/CS, Cu@CMC/PEG, Cu@CMC/EtOH and Cu@CMC/CPE, respectively. It showed that the sutures soaked in a single bath exhibited a shrinking and irregular surface, which could be attributed to the polyelectrolyte reactions between Cu@CMC and CS, the weak interactions of PEG and rapid dehydration of EtOH. Moreover, the SEM image shown in Fig. S1 suggested that PEG could be easily desquamated. The Cu@CMC/CPE sutures have a smooth, uniform surface (Fig. 3(d)) as well as stodgy in cross section (Fig. S2). The result demonstrated that the interactions between the -COOH group on Cu@CMC surface and the -NH2 group on CS exotheca. The CS network could capture an amount of PEG, which account for the smoother surface and the better structure. This was consistent with the work of Mealy et al. [36]. Additionally, to test the entangled chain network between CS and PEG chains, the CS and PEG solutions were contaminated by the Cd (NO3)·4H2O and Pb (NO3)2, respectively. The coated shell in cross section and the elemental mapping in tested were shown in Fig. 3(f–h). These mapping results clearly confirmed that Cu was uniformly disturbed over the sutures, while Cd and Pb were only uniformly disturbed over the outer shell. All results showed that the Cu@CMC/CPE sutures were successfully synthesized and met the requirements in morphology of surgery.

As presented in Fig. 4(a), the typical fluorescence images of live/dead cells (L-929) are characterized. Apoptotic cells could be hardly found when the concentration of Cu-fiber was less than or equal to 0.5 mg·mL−1 while the density of apoptotic cells was enhanced with the increase of Cu-fiber's concentration. The cell viability was shown in Fig. 4(b). The results of the cell viability strongly confirmed that the cell viability values of L-929 was decreased with the increasement of Cu-fiber, but the value still over 80 % when the concentration was 5 mg·mL−1. All the results demonstrated that the Cu-fiber might possess a positive cytocompatibility.

Hemolysis tests in vitro were employed to evaluate the blood compatibility of Cu-fiber. As presented in Fig. 4(c), the appearance of experiment groups was similar to the negative group. The dosage of Cu-fiber had little effect on the hemolysis ratio. The hemolysis ratio increased as the concentration increased, even though at 1600 g mL−1, the percent hemolysis was 4.3 %. It suggested that there was little toxicity on blood cells [38]. All the results indicated that the Cu-fiber was safe as a product in contact with blood.

As the rapid release of Cu2+ may do damage to tissues, the accumulative releasing concentration of Cu2+ was tested. The data in Fig. 4(d) was the slow-release curve of Cu2+, the concentration of Cu2+ was rising as time went by. This release went at the same rate every day, and then reached the equilibrium (<5 mg·mL−1 on the 10th day), which was far below the maximum daily intake recommendation for adults of 2–3 mg according to the World Health Organization [39]. Furthermore, the release of Cu2+ was beneficial for wound healing in early period, because Cu2+ could up-regulate the expression of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF-A), which was a stimulus for angiogenesis, and could speed up the wound healing process [40]. It was also reported that VEGF-A could stimulate keratinocytes to form the epidermal barrier in early period, thereby enhancing re-epithelialization activity [40]. Therefore, Cu-fiber did no damage to the tissues and might accelerate wound healing process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo incisional repair using Cu-fiber
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the potential application in patching treatment, Cu-fibers were applied to mice liver injury, vascular injury and skin injury. Be similar to the stretched Cu@CMC hydrogel, Cu-fibers could absorb tissue fluid and be hydrated, seamlessly seal the needle passages (Fig. S3). The elastic modulus of jugular vein was much higher than that of the liver, so we applied the Cu-fiber into blind jugular vein model. When it was applied to the incision, Cu-fibers got through the vascular easily and did not cause secondary damage to the jugular vein according to the images of vascular closure shown in Fig. S4. Furthermore, Cu-fiber was contrasted with two commercial materials: Nylon and PLGA fibers, and the reparative ability was assessed using a rat full-thickness dorsal skin model. The skins were treated with different materials, as shown in Fig. 5(a). It could be observed that all of the materials displayed the significant mechanical strength to cut the tissues, there were no dehiscence after surgical closure. The good stitching results suggested that Cu-fiber had potential applications in a good deal of tissue fixation and repair.

To evaluate the influence of materials on skin wound healing ability, the skins in incision region were removed all of the suture knots and harvested with staining of H & E and Masson on the postoperative day 1, 4 and 7 (Fig. 5(b)–(c)). When using the rigid dry materials (Nylon) and the absorbable materials (PLGA) to sew up the skin injury, it showed clearly that the skin surrounding the incision region exhibited an unhealed condition on day 4 (Fig. 5b(iv) and (v)). As shown in Fig. 5b(vii) and (viii), the epidermis of all groups was regenerated while stoma did not revert to the original condition. By contrast, the epidermis was completely healed by the Cu-fiber stitching on the 4th day after the closure of the incision (Fig. 5b(ix)), on the day 7, the stoma was almost regenerated in the Cu-fiber treated group (Fig. 5c(ix)). Fig. 5c(vi) and (ix) showed the process of the increasing collagen fibers (stained blue) and the thickening of keratinized (red margin), indicating the facilitation of wound healing. In Fig. 5d, representative digital images of stained cross-section of the heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney sections showed no pathological symptoms, the results showed that the Cu-fiber had no acute toxicity on mice. In addition, the symptoms of diarrhea, acute toxicity or allergic were not observed during the treatment.

Excessive inflammation would lead to abnormal repair in the healing process, IL-1a [28], IL-6 [27,41] and IL-10 [42] have been reported that the inflammatory related cytokines could cause inflammatory or anti-inflammatory effects. To evaluate the immune response in materials-treated mice, the cytokines in skins at incision region on day 7 were studied by qRT-PCR assay. The expression of the inflammatory factors in the PLGA group and Cu-fiber group was normalized relative to the Nylon group. As shown in Fig. 6 , the expression levels of the IL-1a, IL-6 AND IL-10 in Nylon group were much higher than those in the PLGA and Cu-fiber group, indicating that nylon sutures were stimulus for skin tissues which were completely recovered and hard to eliminate the inflammation caused by the substantial mechanical mismatch. In contrast to the rigid dry nylon suture, the softer Cu-fiber decreased the expression levels of inflammatory factors (IL-1a and IL-6), whereas those expression levels in the PLGA group were lower than those of the Cu-fiber group. Interestingly, the expression of IL-10 in Nylon group was higher than those of PLGA and Cu-fiber group, which might be attributed that the Nylon group was still in the inflammatory phase of healing, while the other two groups were in process of proliferation or remodeling [43]. These results revealed that the Cu-fiber works better than the nylon suture on the inhibition of inflammation. However, its effects are unclear and further research is needed in the future.

In this study, we synthesized a mechanically matched hydrogel suture via an easily implementable, safe and effective soaking strategy. CS was employed to interact with Cu@CMC hydrogel sheath constructed by the dynamic cross-linking through electrostatic interactions. PEG enhanced the toughness by improving the stress of suture, while 85 % (V/V) of EtOH washed out the excessive PEG, which ameliorated the defects caused by CS microcrystal, then washed the excessive PEG off. The Cu-fiber constructed by this strategy could close the vascular injury, liver injury and dorsal injury. In the full-thickness dorsal injury, Cu-fiber could promote wound healing and inhibit inflammatory response. All the results indicated that the Cu-fiber fabricated by this strategy was promising in wound healing field.

Yimei Yang: Investigation, Data curation, Software, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Tian Hu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hongyan Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Bu-Ning Liu: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Jinwen Liu: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Wei-Xiong Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Kui Song: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Gang-Biao Jiang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support for this study by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (41977127, 41671302) and Innovation Platform and Talent Program of Hunan Province (2021SK4050).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2022.10.141.
